metadata.title: DENSITY OF ALGEBRAIC POINTS ON PRODUCTS OF CURVES
metadata.abstract: > Abstract. In this paper, we initiate the systematic study of density of algebraic points on surfaces. We give an effective asymptotic range in which the density degree set has regular behavior dictated by the index. By contrast, in small degree, the question of density is subtle and depends on the arithmetic of the curves. We give several explicit examples displaying these different behaviors, including products of genus 2 curves with and without dense quadratic points. These results for products of curves have applications to questions about algebraic points on closely related surfaces, such as rank growth on abelian surfaces and bielliptic surfaces.
metadata.introduction:
Let _X_ be a smooth, projective, and geometrically connected scheme defined over a number field _k_ . We will refer to such a scheme as a nice variety. Write _δ_ ( _X/k_ ) for the density degree set of _X/k_ : the set of natural numbers _d_ for which the closed points of degree _d_ are Zariski dense in _X_ . We denote by N the set of all positive integers. For a nice curve _C_ of genus _g_ , the structure of the set _δ_ ( _C/k_ ) is known to reflect the geometry of _C_ . For instance, min _δ_ ( _C/k_ ) is related to the gonality of _C_ [17, Corollary 5.2.2], and 

(1) 

_δ_ ( _C/k_ ) _∩_ N _≥_ 2 _g_ = ind( _C/k_ )N _∩_ N _≥_ 2 _g,_ 

where ind( _C/k_ ) denotes the index of _C_ [17, Proposition 5.1.1]. 

Far less is known when the dimension of _X_ is at least 2. In this paper, we initiate the study of Zariski density of algebraic points on surfaces in a case that builds upon our understanding for curves. The primary focus is the product of two nice curves _X_ = _C × D_ over _k_ . The structure of _δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) is constrained by the structures of _δ_ ( _C/k_ ) and _δ_ ( _D/k_ ): if the genus of one of _C_ or _D_ is at most 9 we show that 

(2) 

_δ_ ( _C/k_ ) _· δ_ ( _D/k_ ) _⊆ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) _⊆ δ_ ( _C/k_ ) _∩ δ_ ( _D/k_ ) _._ 

For curves of arbitrary genera, the upper bound still holds but the lower bound depends on degrees of the P[1] -parameterized points (see Proposition 2.13). 

Asymptotically, it is known that for any nice variety _X_ , the density degree set _δ_ ( _X/k_ ) contains all sufficiently large multiplies of ind( _X/k_ ) [17, Proposition B.0.1], though this result is not effective. Our first main result is an analogue of (1) for _X_ = _C × D_ . For simplicity, we state the result in the case that _C_ and _D_ both have a _k_ -point; the general result is Theorem 3.6. 

**Theorem 1.1** (Corollary 3.3) **.** _Let C and D be nice curves over k of genera gC and gD. Assume further that C and D both have k-rational points, then_ 


For _gC, gD ≤_ 2, we can sharpen this containment using the explicit geometry of low genus curves. As above, we state our result when _C_ and _D_ both have a _k_ -point. 

**Theorem 1.2** (Theorem 4.9, Theorem 5.8, Proposition 6.1) **.** _Suppose that C and D are nice curves of genera gC and gD and assume that C and D both have k-rational points._ 

_(i) If gC_ = _gD_ = 1 _, then_ N _\ {_ 1 _,_ 2 _} ⊆ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) _⊆_ N _._ 

_(ii) If gC_ = 1 _and gD_ = 2 _, then_ N _≥_ 2 _\ {_ 2 _,_ 3 _,_ 5 _,_ 7 _} ⊆ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) _⊆_ N _≥_ 2 _._ 

_(iii) If gC_ = _gD_ = 2 _, then_ N _≥_ 2 _\ {_ 2 _,_ 3 _,_ 5 _,_ 6 _,_ 7 _,_ 9 _,_ 11 _} ⊆ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) _⊆_ N _≥_ 2 _._ 1 

J. BERG, Y. FU, E. GAZAKI, M. PORZIO, J. RAWSON, AND I. VOGT 

2 

_Remark_ 1.3 _._ In each case of Theorem 1.2, the results can be sharpened further with additional assumptions on the curves. 

If _C_ and _D_ are elliptic curves, we show in Corollary 4.2 that 1 _∈ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) if and only if both curves have positive rank. In Theorem 4.3 we show that 2 _∈ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) provided, for instance, that one of _C_ or _D_ has _j_ -invariant different from 0 _,_ 1728. 

In Theorem 1.2 part (ii), if _C_ is an isogeny factor of the Jacobian of _D_ , we show in Proposition 2.15 that _δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) = _δ_ ( _D/k_ ). Further results depend on whether _D_ has a rational Weierstrass point (see Proposition 5.7 and Theorem 5.8). 

Finally, when both curves have genus 2, our general bounds depend on the index of each of the curves and whether the corresponding product surface has index 1, 2 or 4 (cf. Proposition 6.1 and Corollary 6.3). 

To prove Theorem 1.1, we construct explicit collections of curves _Xγ_ of bounded genus in _C × D_ passing through fixed sets of closed points, and such that their union is dense in _C × D_ . Consequently, applying the bound (1) on each curve, we conclude that for any _d ≥_ 2 _g_ ( _Xγ_ ), the surface has dense degree _d_ points. To prove Theorem 1.2, we both construct lower genus families and exploit the fact that (1) can be sharpened with more explicit knowledge of the divisors on _Xγ_ . 

1.1. **Small Degree Points.** The method of covering the surface with curves and applying the previous strategy, which relies on the Riemann–Roch Theorem, is impractical or impossible for points of very small degree. Instead, we construct these points by pulling back points from other varieties, which requires proving density of rational points on surfaces. This is quite difficult and entangled with several deep conjectures in number theory. For example, we show in Theorem 5.10 that the Parity Conjecture sometimes implies that 3 _∈ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) when _gC_ = 1 and _gD_ = 2. 

For quadratic points on _C ×D_ with _gC, gD ≤_ 2, we consider the quotient surface _S_ := ( _C ×D_ ) _/ι_ , where _ι_ is a hyperelliptic involution. Since _C ×D → S_ is a finite map of degree 2, if 1 _̸∈ δ_ ( _C ×D/k_ ) and 1 _∈ δ_ ( _S/k_ ), then we can conclude via pullback that 2 _∈ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ). (In fact, if Pic[0] _C_[and] Pic[0] _D_[both][have][rank][0,][the][density][of][quadratic][points][on] _[C][ ×][ D]_[can][be][reduced][to][the][density] of rational points on finitely many surfaces of the form ( _C × D_ ) _/ι_ .) 

In low genus, the surface _S_ has interesting geometry. When _C_ and _D_ are elliptic curves, _S_ is the Kummer _K_ 3 surface associated to the abelian surface _C × D_ . When _gC_ = 1 _, gD_ = 2, _S_ is a quadratic twist elliptic fibration over P[1] . Conjecturally, all such surfaces have dense rational points [5, Conjecture 1.1]. In Section 5.1 we give some examples when this conjecture can be verified. 

Lastly, when both _C_ and _D_ have genus 2, the surface _S_ is of general type, and the BombieriLang conjecture predicts that it does not have dense rational points. In Proposition 6.10, we give an example, based on an idea of Adam Logan, of genus 2 curves _C_ and _D_ with _δ_ ( _C/k_ ) = _δ_ ( _D/k_ ) = 2N for which 2 _∈/ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) despite the fact that there exist degree 2 points on _C × D_ . 

On the other hand, when Pic[0] _C_[or][Pic][0] _D_[has][positive][rank,][there][can][be][additional][sources][of] quadratic points. For example, we show in Proposition 6.5 that the quadratic points are dense on a self-product _C × C_ of a genus 2 curve with Pic[0] _C_[of][positive][rank][by][pulling][back][rational] points from Pic[0] _C_[.] 

Some of the arithmetic complexity is eased by considering the potential density degree set. Recall that for a variety _X/k_ , an integer _d_ is in the potential density degree set _℘_ ( _X/k_ ) if there exists a finite extension _k[′] /k_ for which _d ∈ δ_ ( _X/k[′]_ ). If _X_ = _C × D_ is a product of curves and _C_ has genus 0 or 1, we have _℘_ ( _C × D/k_ ) = _℘_ ( _D/k_ ). In the case of products of genus 2 curves, we give a nearly complete description of the potential density degree set in Theorem 6.15. 

1.2. **Applications to non-product surfaces.** One of the main reasons for studying product surfaces is their close relation to the more well-studied setting of curves. Another reason is their closeness to several other interesting families of surfaces. Our methods and results can be applied to principally polarized abelian surfaces and to biellliptic surfaces in particular. 

DENSITY OF ALGEBRAIC POINTS ON PRODUCTS OF CURVES 

3 

In both cases, we make use of the fact they are covered by a product of curves. One subtlety is that the image of a degree _d_ point under a finite map may be a smaller degree point. To circumvent this issue, we utilize constructions of points where we can guarantee that the residue field is a primitive extension of _k_ . 

**Theorem 1.4** (cf. Theorem 6.4, Theorem 7.3) **.** _If A/k is an abelian surface that is isogenous (over k) to a principally polarized abelian surface, then_ 


_If, furthermore, A is isogenous to the Jacobian of a genus_ 2 _curve C/k, then_ 2 _∈ δ_ ( _A/k_ ) _._ 

These density statements for abelian surfaces can be re-interpreted as statements about the behavior of ranks of these abelian varieties over field extensions (see Corollary 4.10). This interpretation is one of the key steps for the following description of the density degree set of a bielliptic surface. 

**Theorem 1.5** (Corollary 4.13) **.** _Let S_ = ( _E_ 1 _× E_ 2) _/G be a bielliptic surface over k, where G acts on E_ 1 _via translations by k-rational points and on E_ 2 _such that E_ 2 _/G ≃_ P[1] _. Then,_ 


_Remark_ 1.6 _._ One of the main properties of the density degree set of curves is that it is closed under multiplication by positive integers [17, Section 5.2.1]. Whether the same property holds for higher dimensional varieties is still unknown. However, in many cases that we consider, our sharper description of the density degree set implies that it satisfies this multiplicativity property. 

1.3. **Outline.** The paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we recall what is known about the density degree sets of curves. We also prove the upper and lower bounds on the density degree set of a product surface given in (2). Section 3 is devoted to describing our general strategy and proving Theorem 1.1. Products of elliptic curves and bielliptic surfaces are discussed in Section 4. Section 5 concerns the product of an elliptic curve and a curve of genus 2. Products of two genus 2 curves, and also Jacobians of genus 2 curves are studied in Section 6. In this section we also study the potential density degree set for products of low genus curves. Section 7 extends the description of the density degree set of a principally polarized abelian surfaces via isogenies. 

1.4. **Notation.** Unless otherwise stated, throughout this paper _k_ will be a number field. All varieties will be defined over _k_ and will be assumed to be nice (i.e. smooth, projective, geometrically connected). The index of a variety _X_ will be denoted by ind( _X/k_ ). 

**Acknowledgments.** This work began at the American Institute of Mathematics workshop “Degree _d_ points on algebraic surfaces” in March 2024. We would like to thank the organizers Nathan Chen and Bianca Viray, as well as all of the staff at AIM and the National Science Foundation for their support. We would also like to thank Niven Achenjang, Adam Logan, Samir Siksek, and Rosa Winter for helpful conversations. E.G. was partially supported by NSF grant DMS-2302196. J.R. was supported by UK EPSRC grant EP/W523793/1. I.V. was supported in part by NSF grants DMS-2200655 and DMS-2338345.

evidence.section: 5. Products of an elliptic curve and genus 2 curve
evidence.original_text: Theorem 5.8. Let E be an elliptic curve of rank 0 and let C be a nice genus 2 curve with C(k) ≠ ∅. Then N≥2 \ {2, 3, 5, 7} ⊆ δ(E × C/k). If C has a k-rational Weierstrass point, then 7 ∈ δ(E × C/k) as well. Proof. Since both E(k) ≠ ∅ and C(k) ≠ ∅, the product E × C has a k-rational point as well. By Corollary 3.3, any integer d greater or equal than N(E, C) = 2((gon(E) − 1)(gon(C) − 1) + gon(E)gC + gon(C)gE) is in δ(E × C/k). By Proposition 2.13 we have that δ(E/k) · δ(C/k) ⊆ δ(E × C/k). Therefore it remains to show that 9, 11 and 13 are in δ(E × C/k), and 7 ∈ δ(E × C/k) if C has a rational Weierstrass point.

evidence.original_text: Proposition 6.1. Let C, D be two nice curves of genus 2 with ind(C/k) = ind(D/k) = 1. Then, according to the arithmetic of C and D, the density set δ(C × D/k) contains the sets listed in Table 1. Proof. First, consider the case when C(k), D(k) = ∅. Corollary 3.3 constructs a family of geometric genus at most 9 curves in C × D, which yields an inclusion N≥18 ⊂ δ(C × D/k). We remark that by the Castelnuovo–Severi inequality, these curves cannot be hyperelliptic as they double cover genus 2 curves. We consider separately the cases where C and D have both rational Weierstrass points. (i) If both C and D have rational Weierstrass points, the covering family generically has a node, and the geometric genus is at most 8. By applying a translation to one of these curves, we may assume that the covering curves are generically smooth away from this identified node, and so have genus exactly 8. By Lemma 3.2, for infinitely many members of this family, the normalization has two rational points over this node, ∞±. In particular, the degree 2 covering map to C is not branched at these points. Since these covering curves are not hyperelliptic, Lemma 2.4 implies that N≥12 is contained within δ(C × D/k). Applying Lemma 2.5 and Remark 2.6 for Z1 = d∞+, for all d < 8 − 2 × 2 = 4, the integer 2 × 8 − 2 − d = 14 − d is in the density degree set. Therefore, we also have 11 ∈ δ(C × D/k).

evidence.original_text: Corollary 6.6. Let C be a genus 2 curve over k. Then 2N≥2 ⊂ δ(C × C/k). If C has index 2 and Pic0C is a simple abelian surface with positive rank over k, then δ(C × C/k) = 2N. In particular, the upper bound of Proposition 2.13 is achieved in this case. Proof. Theorem 6.4 and Proposition 6.5 give 2N≥2 ⊂ δ(C × C/k). Moreover, if Pic0C is simple and rk(Pic0C(k)) > 0, then it follows by Proposition 7.1 that 2 ∈ δ(C × C/k). We conclude that δ(C × C/k) = 2N, which is precisely the upper bound of Proposition 2.13.

evidence.original_text: Definition 2.1. Let X be a nice variety over a field k. The density degree set δ(X/k) is the set of all positive integers d such that the degree d points on X are Zariski dense. Definition 2.2. Let X be a nice variety over a field k. The potential degree set ℘(X/k) is the union of δ(X/k′) as k′/k ranges over all possible finite extensions.

evidence.original_text: The main properties of δ(C/k) that we will need in this article are the following: (1) δ(C/k) is closed under multiplication by positive integers [17, Section 5.2.1]. (2) δ(C/k) contains all multiples of the index of C that are at least max(2g, 1) [17, Proposition 5.1.1]. (3) Every closed point on C of degree at least g + 1 is P1-parameterized [17, Corollary 4.1.2]. (4) If k′/k is a finite extension, then δP1(C/k) ⊂ δP1(C/k′) [17, Lemma 5.5.2].

entries.description: Properties of density degree set δ(C/k) for curves

evidence.original_text: Lemma 3.1. Let C and D be nice curves and let Z ⊂ C × D be an effective zero-cycle with projections ZC ⊂ C and ZD ⊂ D. Let fC : C → P1 and fD : D → P1 be nonconstant morphisms such that fC(ZC red) = fD(ZD red) = ∞ ∈ P1. For any automorphism γ : P1 → P1 fixing ∞, the fiber product Xγ := C ×γ◦fC,fD D is a curve of arithmetic genus (deg(fC) − 1)(deg(fD) − 1) + deg(fC)gD + deg(fD)gC. The inclusion (πC, πD) : Xγ → C × D sends Xγ to a curve on C × D through the zero-cycle Z. Varying γ in any infinite family, this family of curves sweeps out a dense locus in C × D.

entries.description: Standardized construction of covering curves Xγ for product surfaces C × D

evidence.original_text: In this paper, we initiate the systematic study of density of algebraic points on surfaces. We give an effective asymptotic range in which the density degree set has regular behavior dictated by the index. By contrast, in small degree, the question of density is subtle and depends on the arithmetic of the curves. We give several explicit examples displaying these different behaviors, including products of genus 2 curves with and without dense quadratic points. These results for products of curves have applications to questions about algebraic points on closely related surfaces, such as rank growth on abelian surfaces and bielliptic surfaces.

Far less is known when the dimension of X is at least 2. In this paper, we initiate the study of Zariski density of algebraic points on surfaces in a case that builds upon our understanding for curves. The primary focus is the product of two nice curves X = C × D over k. The structure of δ(C × D/k) is constrained by the structures of δ(C/k) and δ(D/k): if the genus of one of C or D is at most 9 we show that δ(C/k) · δ(D/k) ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ δ(C/k) ∩ δ(D/k). For curves of arbitrary genera, the upper bound still holds but the lower bound depends on degrees of the P1-parameterized points (see Proposition 2.13).

Asymptotically, it is known that for any nice variety X, the density degree set δ(X/k) contains all sufficiently large multiplies of ind(X/k) [17, Proposition B.0.1], though this result is not effective. Our first main result is an analogue of (1) for X = C × D. For simplicity, we state the result in the case that C and D both have a k-point; the general result is Theorem 3.6. Theorem 1.1 (Corollary 3.3). Let C and D be nice curves over k of genera gC and gD. Assume further that C and D both have k-rational points, then δ(C × D/k) ∩ N≥N(C,D) = ind(C × D/k)N ∩ N≥N(C,D), where N(C,D) = 2((gon(C)−1)(gon(D)−1)+gon(C)gD+gon(D)gC).

For gC, gD ≤ 2, we can sharpen this containment using the explicit geometry of low genus curves. As above, we state our result when C and D both have a k-point. Theorem 1.2 (Theorem 4.9, Theorem 5.8, Proposition 6.1). Suppose that C and D are nice curves of genera gC and gD and assume that C and D both have k-rational points. (i) If gC = gD = 1, then N \ {1,2} ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ N. (ii) If gC = 1 and gD = 2, then N≥2 \ {2,3,5,7} ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ N≥2. (iii) If gC = gD = 2, then N≥2 \ {2,3,5,6,7,9,11} ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ N≥2.

If C and D are elliptic curves, we show in Corollary 4.2 that 1 ∈ δ(C × D/k) if and only if both curves have positive rank. In Theorem 4.3 we show that 2 ∈ δ(C × D/k) provided, for instance, that one of C or D has j-invariant different from 0,1728.

In Theorem 1.2 part (ii), if C is an isogeny factor of the Jacobian of D, we show in Proposition 2.15 that δ(C × D/k) = δ(D/k). Further results depend on whether D has a rational Weierstrass point (see Proposition 5.7 and Theorem 5.8). Finally, when both curves have genus 2, our general bounds depend on the index of each of the curves and whether the corresponding product surface has index 1, 2 or 4 (cf. Proposition 6.1 and Corollary 6.3).

To prove Theorem 1.1, we construct explicit collections of curves Xγ of bounded genus in C × D passing through fixed sets of closed points, and such that their union is dense in C × D. Consequently, applying the bound (1) on each curve, we conclude that for any d ≥ 2g(Xγ), the surface has dense degree d points. To prove Theorem 1.2, we both construct lower genus families and exploit the fact that (1) can be sharpened with more explicit knowledge of the divisors on Xγ.

For quadratic points on C ×D with gC, gD ≤ 2, we consider the quotient surface S := (C ×D)/ι, where ι is a hyperelliptic involution. Since C ×D → S is a finite map of degree 2, if 1 ̸∈ δ(C ×D/k) and 1 ∈ δ(S/k), then we can conclude via pullback that 2 ∈ δ(C × D/k). (In fact, if Pic0C and Pic0D both have rank 0, the density of quadratic points on C × D can be reduced to the density of rational points on finitely many surfaces of the form (C × D)/ι.)

In low genus, the surface S has interesting geometry. When C and D are elliptic curves, S is the Kummer K3 surface associated to the abelian surface C × D. When gC = 1, gD = 2, S is a quadratic twist elliptic fibration over P1. Conjecturally, all such surfaces have dense rational points [5, Conjecture 1.1]. In Section 5.1 we give some examples when this conjecture can be verified. Lastly, when both C and D have genus 2, the surface S is of general type, and the BombieriLang conjecture predicts that it does not have dense rational points. In Proposition 6.10, we give an example, based on an idea of Adam Logan, of genus 2 curves C and D with δ(C/k) = δ(D/k) = 2N for which 2 ∈/ δ(C × D/k) despite the fact that there exist degree 2 points on C × D.

On the other hand, when Pic0C or Pic0D has positive rank, there can be additional sources of quadratic points. For example, we show in Proposition 6.5 that the quadratic points are dense on a self-product C × C of a genus 2 curve with Pic0C of positive rank by pulling back rational points from Pic0C.

Some of the arithmetic complexity is eased by considering the potential density degree set. Recall that for a variety X/k, an integer d is in the potential density degree set ℘(X/k) if there exists a finite extension k′/k for which d ∈ δ(X/k′). If X = C × D is a product of curves and C has genus 0 or 1, we have ℘(C × D/k) = ℘(D/k). In the case of products of genus 2 curves, we give a nearly complete description of the potential density degree set in Theorem 6.15.

One of the main reasons for studying product surfaces is their close relation to the more well-studied setting of curves. Another reason is their closeness to several other interesting families of surfaces. Our methods and results can be applied to principally polarized abelian surfaces and to biellliptic surfaces in particular. In both cases, we make use of the fact they are covered by a product of curves. One subtlety is that the image of a degree d point under a finite map may be a smaller degree point. To circumvent this issue, we utilize constructions of points where we can guarantee that the residue field is a primitive extension of k.

Theorem 1.4 (cf. Theorem 6.4, Theorem 7.3). If A/k is an abelian surface that is isogenous (over k) to a principally polarized abelian surface, then δ(A/k) ∩ N≥N(A) = ind(A/k)N ∩ N≥N(A). If, furthermore, A is isogenous to the Jacobian of a genus 2 curve C/k, then 2 ∈ δ(A/k).

These density statements for abelian surfaces can be re-interpreted as statements about the behavior of ranks of these abelian varieties over field extensions (see Corollary 4.10). This interpretation is one of the key steps for the following description of the density degree set of a bielliptic surface. Theorem 1.5 (Corollary 4.13). Let S = (E1 × E2)/G be a bielliptic surface over k, where G acts on E1 via translations by k-rational points and on E2 such that E2/G ≃ P1. Then, δ(S/k) ⊆ δ(E1/k) and N≥3 ∪ {4} ⊆ δ(S/k).

Asymptotically, it is known that for any nice variety X, the density degree set δ(X/k) contains all sufficiently large multiplies of ind(X/k) [17, Proposition B.0.1], though this result is not effective. Our first main result is an analogue of (1) for X = C × D. For simplicity, we state the result in the case that C and D both have a k-point; the general result is Theorem 3.6. Theorem 1.1 (Corollary 3.3). Let C and D be nice curves over k of genera gC and gD. Assume further that C and D both have k-rational points, then δ(C × D/k) ∩ N≥N(C,D) = ind(C × D/k)N ∩ N≥N(C,D), where N(C,D) = 2((gon(C)−1)(gon(D)−1)+gon(C)gD+gon(D)gC).

For gC, gD ≤ 2, we can sharpen this containment using the explicit geometry of low genus curves. As above, we state our result when C and D both have a k-point. Theorem 1.2 (Theorem 4.9, Theorem 5.8, Proposition 6.1). Suppose that C and D are nice curves of genera gC and gD and assume that C and D both have k-rational points. (i) If gC = gD = 1, then N \ {1,2} ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ N. (ii) If gC = 1 and gD = 2, then N≥2 \ {2,3,5,7} ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ N≥2. (iii) If gC = gD = 2, then N≥2 \ {2,3,5,6,7,9,11} ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ N≥2.

If C and D are elliptic curves, we show in Corollary 4.2 that 1 ∈ δ(C × D/k) if and only if both curves have positive rank. In Theorem 4.3 we show that 2 ∈ δ(C × D/k) provided, for instance, that one of C or D has j-invariant different from 0,1728.

In Theorem 1.2 part (ii), if C is an isogeny factor of the Jacobian of D, we show in Proposition 2.15 that δ(C × D/k) = δ(D/k). Further results depend on whether D has a rational Weierstrass point (see Proposition 5.7 and Theorem 5.8). Finally, when both curves have genus 2, our general bounds depend on the index of each of the curves and whether the corresponding product surface has index 1, 2 or 4 (cf. Proposition 6.1 and Corollary 6.3).

To prove Theorem 1.1, we construct explicit collections of curves Xγ of bounded genus in C × D passing through fixed sets of closed points, and such that their union is dense in C × D. Consequently, applying the bound (1) on each curve, we conclude that for any d ≥ 2g(Xγ), the surface has dense degree d points. To prove Theorem 1.2, we both construct lower genus families and exploit the fact that (1) can be sharpened with more explicit knowledge of the divisors on Xγ.

For quadratic points on C ×D with gC, gD ≤ 2, we consider the quotient surface S := (C ×D)/ι, where ι is a hyperelliptic involution. Since C ×D → S is a finite map of degree 2, if 1 ̸∈ δ(C ×D/k) and 1 ∈ δ(S/k), then we can conclude via pullback that 2 ∈ δ(C × D/k). (In fact, if Pic0C and Pic0D both have rank 0, the density of quadratic points on C × D can be reduced to the density of rational points on finitely many surfaces of the form (C × D)/ι.)

In low genus, the surface S has interesting geometry. When C and D are elliptic curves, S is the Kummer K3 surface associated to the abelian surface C × D. When gC = 1, gD = 2, S is a quadratic twist elliptic fibration over P1. Conjecturally, all such surfaces have dense rational points [5, Conjecture 1.1]. In Section 5.1 we give some examples when this conjecture can be verified. Lastly, when both C and D have genus 2, the surface S is of general type, and the BombieriLang conjecture predicts that it does not have dense rational points. In Proposition 6.10, we give an example, based on an idea of Adam Logan, of genus 2 curves C and D with δ(C/k) = δ(D/k) = 2N for which 2 ∈/ δ(C × D/k) despite the fact that there exist degree 2 points on C × D.

On the other hand, when Pic0C or Pic0D has positive rank, there can be additional sources of quadratic points. For example, we show in Proposition 6.5 that the quadratic points are dense on a self-product C × C of a genus 2 curve with Pic0C of positive rank by pulling back rational points from Pic0C.

Some of the arithmetic complexity is eased by considering the potential density degree set. Recall that for a variety X/k, an integer d is in the potential density degree set ℘(X/k) if there exists a finite extension k′/k for which d ∈ δ(X/k′). If X = C × D is a product of curves and C has genus 0 or 1, we have ℘(C × D/k) = ℘(D/k). In the case of products of genus 2 curves, we give a nearly complete description of the potential density degree set in Theorem 6.15.

One of the main reasons for studying product surfaces is their close relation to the more well-studied setting of curves. Another reason is their closeness to several other interesting families of surfaces. Our methods and results can be applied to principally polarized abelian surfaces and to biellliptic surfaces in particular. In both cases, we make use of the fact they are covered by a product of curves. One subtlety is that the image of a degree d point under a finite map may be a smaller degree point. To circumvent this issue, we utilize constructions of points where we can guarantee that the residue field is a primitive extension of k.

Theorem 1.4 (cf. Theorem 6.4, Theorem 7.3). If A/k is an abelian surface that is isogenous (over k) to a principally polarized abelian surface, then δ(A/k) ∩ N≥N(A) = ind(A/k)N ∩ N≥N(A). If, furthermore, A is isogenous to the Jacobian of a genus 2 curve C/k, then 2 ∈ δ(A/k).

These density statements for abelian surfaces can be re-interpreted as statements about the behavior of ranks of these abelian varieties over field extensions (see Corollary 4.10). This interpretation is one of the key steps for the following description of the density degree set of a bielliptic surface. Theorem 1.5 (Corollary 4.13). Let S = (E1 × E2)/G be a bielliptic surface over k, where G acts on E1 via translations by k-rational points and on E2 such that E2/G ≃ P1. Then, δ(S/k) ⊆ δ(E1/k) and N≥3 ∪ {4} ⊆ δ(S/k).

In this paper, we initiate the systematic study of density of algebraic points on surfaces. We give an effective asymptotic range in which the density degree set has regular behavior dictated by the index. By contrast, in small degree, the question of density is subtle and depends on the arithmetic of the curves. We give several explicit examples displaying these different behaviors, including products of genus 2 curves with and without dense quadratic points. These results for products of curves have applications to questions about algebraic points on closely related surfaces, such as rank growth on abelian surfaces and bielliptic surfaces.
